summary: This article explores Rake, a Ruby-based build language that serves as an internal DSL for dependency-based programming. Fowler explains how Rake differs from make and ant by leveraging Ruby's full programming capabilities while maintaining readable build syntax. He covers file tasks, synthesizing dependencies, platform-specific XML processing, and debugging techniques. The article demonstrates how Rake's flexible dependency specification and Ruby integration enable cleaner, more maintainable build scripts than external DSLs can provide.
key_points: Build languages like Rake use dependency-based computation rather than imperative style, which naturally fits build processes and avoids redundant task execution
Rake being an internal DSL means you get the full power of Ruby when needed, unlike ant and make which require dropping out of the DSL for complex logic
File tasks provide timestamp-based necessity checking similar to make, only rebuilding when outputs are older than inputs
Dependencies can be specified flexibly - either at the dependent task, the prerequisite task, or independently - enabling cleaner organization
The ability to synthesize tasks programmatically using Ruby loops and FileList eliminates tedious manual enumeration of source files
money_quotes: The fact that rake is an internal DSL for a general purpose language is a very important difference between it and the other two. It essentially allows me to use the full power of ruby any time I need it.
I've come to view that a build language is really ideally suited to an internal DSL because you do need that full language power just often enough to make it worthwhile.
I suspect that few people that knock up a build script realize they are programming in a funky computational model, but that's what it is.
Often when you come across something new it can be a good idea to overuse it in order to find out its boundaries.
The lesson from this is that with this kind of internal DSL you have the ability to interrogate the object structure to figure out what's going on.
After all, until we tried it I thought XML would be a good syntax for build files.
Sub-routines are one of the most elementary ways of structuring a program, and the lack of convenient sub-routine mechanisms is one of the great problems of ant and make.
key_insight: Build languages are most powerful when implemented as internal DSLs within full programming languages, because build automation frequently requires general-purpose programming capabilities that external DSLs cannot elegantly provide.
